text:
CT CHEST / ABDOMEN / PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
POST-TRANSPLANT SURVEILLANCE STUDY

CLINICAL INDICATION:
Renata Vasquez-Okonkwo is a 48-year-old female with a history of AML (FLT3-ITD high allelic ratio, NPM1 wild-type, trisomy 8, ELN 2022 adverse risk) who underwent myeloablative conditioning allogeneic peripheral blood stem cell transplantation from a 10/10 HLA-matched unrelated donor approximately 90 days prior to this examination. She is now approximately day +90 post-transplant. Study is obtained for post-transplant surveillance to assess for extramedullary leukemic relapse, myeloid sarcoma, infectious or inflammatory pulmonary complications, and evaluation of abdominal organ reconstitution following prior documented hepato-splenomegaly. Recent acute GVHD (Grade II, skin and gastrointestinal involvement) has been treated with systemic methylprednisolone; patient currently tapering steroids. On letermovir, acyclovir, and trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole prophylaxis.

COMPARISON:
CT chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast performed approximately 6 weeks prior to transplant (pre-transplant workup study). Spleen measured 10.8 cm at that time (down from 16.4 cm at initial presentation).

TECHNIQUE:
Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis acquired following administration of 120 mL of intravenous iodinated contrast (Omnipaque 350) in the portal venous phase. Axial images obtained at 5 mm slice thickness; coronal and sagittal reformats reviewed. Dose modulation applied.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and Airways:
The central airways are patent. There is a subtle area of hazy ground-glass density in the posterior right lower lobe, measuring approximately 1.2 cm in greatest dimension, without associated consolidation, bronchial wall thickening, or air bronchograms. No pleural-based component. The margins are ill-defined and the opacity is non-mass-like. The appearance is most consistent with a resolving infectious or inflammatory focus; aspiration cannot be entirely excluded given the posterior location. No additional ground-glass changes are identified elsewhere in either lung. No centrilobular nodules or tree-in-bud opacities to suggest an endobronchial infectious process. No pulmonary nodules or masses. No cavitary lesions. Lungs are otherwise clear bilaterally.

Pleura:
No pleural effusions bilaterally. No pleural thickening.

Mediastinum and Hila:
The mediastinal and hilar contours are unremarkable. No mediastinal widening. No anterior mediastinal mass. No paratracheal, precarinal, subcarinal, hilar, or axillary lymphadenopathy. The largest visualized mediastinal node measures approximately 6 mm in short axis (not enlarged by size criteria, and within expected post-transplant reactive range).

Pericardium and Heart:
Normal cardiac size and configuration. No pericardial effusion. No pericardial thickening.

Chest Wall and Osseous Structures:
No pathologic fractures or destructive osseous lesions. No extramedullary soft tissue masses involving the chest wall or paravertebral regions.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver:
The liver is normal in size, with a craniocaudal span of approximately 14.3 cm (previously 17.1 cm at initial presentation, and not specifically measured on pre-transplant study but noted to have resolved hepatomegaly). Hepatic parenchymal attenuation is homogeneous on portal venous phase imaging. No focal hepatic lesions. No biliary ductal dilation. The hepatic vasculature appears patent. No periportal edema or features suggestive of sinusoidal obstruction syndrome (SOS/VOD), which had been a consideration given busulfan conditioning.

Spleen:
The spleen is normal in size, measuring approximately 10.2 cm in craniocaudal dimension (previously 10.8 cm on pre-transplant imaging, 16.4 cm at initial presentation). The splenic parenchyma is homogeneous without focal lesions, infarcts, or mass effect. No perisplenic fluid. Complete interval resolution of the previously documented splenomegaly.

Pancreas:
The pancreas is normal in size, contour, and attenuation. No pancreatic ductal dilation. No peripancreatic fluid or inflammatory changes.

Kidneys and Adrenal Glands:
Bilateral kidneys are normal in size and enhancement pattern. No hydronephrosis. No renal masses. No perirenal fluid collections. Adrenal glands are normal bilaterally without nodularity or thickening, reassuring in the context of ongoing steroid therapy.

Lymph Nodes (Abdomen):
No pathologic para-aortic, aortocaval, mesenteric, or retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy. No bulky nodal disease. Scattered small mesenteric lymph nodes are noted, the largest measuring approximately 7 mm in short axis, consistent with reactive adenopathy in the setting of recent immune activation and gastrointestinal GVHD.

Bowel:
The visualized small and large bowel loops are within normal caliber without wall thickening or mesenteric fat stranding. No obstruction. No pneumatosis. Findings are reassuring given recent history of gastrointestinal GVHD-related diarrhea (now clinically resolving).

Ascites:
No free fluid in the abdomen or pelvis.

Extramedullary Disease Assessment:
No soft tissue masses, enhancing nodal conglomerates, or infiltrative lesions suggestive of myeloid sarcoma or chloroma in the abdomen or pelvis. No paraspinal masses. No organ infiltration with abnormal soft tissue density that would raise concern for extramedullary AML involvement.

---

PELVIS:

Pelvic Lymph Nodes:
No inguinal, iliac, or obturator lymphadenopathy.

Pelvic Organs:
Uterus and adnexa are grossly unremarkable given limited evaluation without dedicated pelvic protocol. No pelvic free fluid. No pelvic masses.

Bones:
No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions. No cortical destruction. The visible lumbar spine, sacrum, and pelvic bones appear unremarkable without marrow-replacing lesions.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. Small, ill-defined ground-glass opacity in the posterior right lower lobe measuring approximately 1.2 cm. The appearance is most in keeping with a resolving infectious or inflammatory process in the context of recent febrile neutropenia and post-transplant immunosuppression; aspiration is also possible given the dependent posterior distribution. No features of consolidation, cavitation, or nodular architecture to suggest fungal or other opportunistic infection at this time. Clinical and microbiologic correlation recommended; follow-up imaging to confirm resolution is appropriate.

2. Complete interval resolution of the previously documented hepatomegaly and splenomegaly. Spleen now normal in size at 10.2 cm; liver measures 14.3 cm in craniocaudal span with homogeneous parenchyma. No features of sinusoidal obstruction syndrome.

3. No lymphadenopathy above or below the diaphragm. No hepatic, splenic, or other visceral focal lesions.

4. No imaging evidence of extramedullary leukemic disease. No masses, infiltrative lesions, or soft tissue densities to suggest myeloid sarcoma or chloromatous involvement of any organ, soft tissue, or bony structure.

5. No pleural or pericardial effusion.

6. Small scattered mesenteric lymph nodes, likely reactive in the setting of recent gastrointestinal GVHD; not pathologic by morphologic criteria.

Overall, this post-transplant surveillance study is reassuring with no radiographic evidence of leukemic relapse or extramedullary disease. The solitary right lower lobe ground-glass opacity warrants monitoring.
